findings.title: HQ dashboard injects unescaped product names into `innerHTML`
findings.description: The HQ dashboard defines an `esc()` helper but bypasses it when rendering product options. `product.key` and `product.name` are inserted directly into `select.innerHTML`, so a malicious value in `org-data.json` can inject markup or script-bearing attributes into the page.
findings.impact: A poisoned dashboard data file can execute JavaScript in the admin dashboard origin, exposing dashboard data and any browser-stored credentials or session state.
findings.fix_suggestion: Build the `<option>` nodes with `document.createElement('option')`, assign `.value` and `.textContent`, or escape both fields before writing HTML.

findings.title: API server accepts arbitrary request bodies with no size limit
findings.description: The API handler trusts `Content-Length` and reads the entire body into memory before parsing JSON. There is no upper bound or early rejection for oversized requests.
findings.impact: A large POST to `/api/run-scan` or `/api/run-all` can force the process to allocate attacker-controlled memory and degrade or crash the scan worker.
findings.fix_suggestion: Reject bodies above a small fixed limit such as 8-16 KB with HTTP 413 before calling `read()`, and handle invalid `Content-Length` values defensively.

findings.title: Production scan API has no rate limiting on expensive POST endpoints
findings.description: Authenticated POST requests go straight from `do_POST()` to scan-launch handlers. There is no per-IP, per-key, or per-department throttling around `/api/run-scan`, `/api/run-all`, or `/api/stop`.
findings.impact: A leaked API key or abusive client can repeatedly trigger costly scans and starve the worker machine, even if only one job per department runs at a time.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add request throttling keyed by client IP and API key, with stricter limits for scan-trigger endpoints and clear 429 responses.

findings.title: API target resolution bypasses configured repository allowlist
findings.description: The production API is documented as config-driven, but `resolve_target()` accepts any existing directory path from the request body before consulting configured targets. That means the caller is not restricted to the repositories listed in `api-config.yaml`.
findings.impact: Any caller with the API key can direct agents at arbitrary directories on the worker host, widening the blast radius from approved repos to local source trees or sensitive filesystem locations.
findings.fix_suggestion: Only resolve targets from the configured `targets` map, or canonicalize and verify requested paths against an explicit allowlist of approved repo roots.

findings.title: Background scan failures are silently discarded by the API server
findings.description: The worker thread runs the department script without `check=True` and does not record the exit status. Regardless of success or failure, the job is removed from `running_jobs`, so callers only see that the scan stopped, not that it failed.
findings.impact: Failed scans can be misreported as completed, which hides regressions from operators and breaks any monitoring that depends on API status alone.
findings.fix_suggestion: Run subprocesses with `check=False`, capture the return code explicitly, and persist a failed job state or error message in the jobs metadata before removing the job from the active set.
